author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm LXXXIII

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Of this Psalm the title is, “A song of a Psalm of Asaph.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We have already often said what is the interpretation of Asaph, that is, congregation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That man, therefore, who was called Asaph, is named in representation of the congregation of God’s people in the titles of many Psalms.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in Greek, congregation is called synagogue, which has come to be held for a kind of proper name for the Jewish people, that it should be called The Synagogue; even as the Christian people is more usually called The Church, in that it too is congregated.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The people of God, then, in this Psalm saith, “O God, who shall be like unto Thee?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which I suppose to be more fitly taken of Christ, because, being made in the likeness of men, He was thought by those by whom He was despised to be comparable to other men: for He was even “reckoned among the unrighteous,” but for this purpose, that He might be judged.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when He shall come to judge, then shall be done what is here said, “O God, who is like unto Thee?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if the Psalms did not use to speak to the Lord Christ, that too would not be spoken which not one of the faithful can doubt was spoken unto Christ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever, a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of Thy kingdom.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To him therefore also now it is said, “O God, who shall be like unto Thee?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For unto many Thou didst vouchsafe to be likened in Thy humiliation, even so far as to the robbers that were crucified with Thee: but when in glory Thou shalt come, “who shall be like unto Thee?”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For lo Thine enemies have sounded, and they that hate Thee have lifted up the head” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He seems to me to signify the last days, when these things that are now repressed by fear are to break forth into free utterance, but quite irrational, so that it should rather be called a “sound,” than speech or discourse.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They will not, therefore, then begin to hate, but “they that hate Thee” will then “lift up the head.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And not “heads,” but “head;” since they are to come even to that point, that they shall have that head, which “is lifted up above all that is called God, and that is worshipped;” so that in him especially is to be fulfilled, “He that exalteth himself shall be abased;” and when He to whom it is said, “Keep not silence, nor grow mild, O God,” shall “slay him with the breath of His mouth, and shall destroy with the brightness of His coming.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Upon Thy people they have malignantly taken counsel” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or, as other copies have it, “They have cunningly devised counsel, and have devised against Thy saints.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In scorn this is said.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For how should they be able to hurt the nation or people of God, or His saints, who know how to say, “If God be for us, who shall be against us?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They have said, Come, and let us destroy them from a nation” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He has put the singular number for the plural: as it is said, “Whose is this cattle,” even though the question be of a flock, and the meaning “these cattle.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lastly, other copies have “from nations,” where the translators have rather followed the sense than the word.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Come, and let us destroy them from a nation.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is that sound whereby they “sounded” rather than spake, since they did vainly make a noise with vain sayings.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And let it not be mentioned of the name of Israel any more.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This others have expressed more plainly, “and let there not be remembrance of the name of Israel any more.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since, “let it be mentioned of the name” (memoretur nominis), is an unusual phrase in the Latin language; for it is rather customary to say, “let the name be mentioned” (memoretur nomen); but the sense is the same.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he who said, “let it be mentioned of the name,” translated the Greek phrase.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But Israel must here be understood in fact of the seed of Abraham, to which the Apostle saith, “Therefore ye are the seed of Abraham, according to the promise heirs.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Not Israel according to the flesh, of which he saith, “Behold Israel after the flesh.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Since they have imagined with one consent; together against Thee have they disposed a testament” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 5): as though they could be the stronger.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In fact, “a testament” is a name given in the Scriptures not only to that which is of no avail till the death of the testators, but every covenant and decree they used to call a testament.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For Laban and Jacob made a testament, which was certainly to have force between the living; and such cases without number are read in the words of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Then he begins to make mention of the enemies of Christ, under certain proper names of nations; the interpretation of which names sufficiently indicates what he would have to be understood.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For by such names are most suitably figured the enemies of the truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Idumæans,” for instance, are interpreted either “men of blood,” or “of earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Ismaelites,” are “obedient to themselves,” and therefore not to God, but to themselves.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Moab,” “from the father;” which in a bad sense has no better explanation, than by considering it so connected with the actual history, that Lot, a father, by the illicit intercourse procured by his daughter, begat him; since it was from that very circumstance he was so named.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Good, however, was his father, but as “the Law is good if one use it lawfully,” not impurely and unlawfully.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Hagarens,” proselytes, that is strangers, by which name also are signified, among the enemies of God’s people, not those who become citizens, but those who persevere in a foreign and alien mind, and when an opportunity of doing harm occurs, show themselves.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Gebal,” “a vain valley,” that is, humble in pretence.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Amon,” “an unquiet people,” or “a people of sadness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Amalech,” “a people licking;” whence elsewhere it is said, “and his enemies shall lick the earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The “alien race,” though by their very name in Latin, they sufficiently show themselves to be aliens, and for this cause of course enemies, yet in the Hebrew are called “Philistines,” which is explained, “falling from drink,” as of persons made drunken by worldly luxury.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Tyre” in Hebrew is called Sor; which whether it be interpreted straitness or tribulation, must be taken in the case of these enemies of God’s people in that sense, of which the Apostle speaks, “Tribulation and straitness on every soul of man that doeth evil.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All these are thus enumerated in the Psalms: “The tabernacles of the Edomites, Ishmaelites, Moab and the Hagarenes, Gebal, and Amon, and Amalech, and the Philistines with those who inhabit Tyre.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And as if to point out the cause why they are enemies of God’s people, he adds, “For Assur came with them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now Assur is often used figuratively for the devil, “who works in the children of disobedience,” as in his own vessels, that they may assail the people of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They have holpen the children of Lot,” he saith: for all enemies, by the working in them of the devil, their prince, “have holpen the children of Lot,” who is explained to mean “one declining.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the apostate angels are well explained as the children of declension, for by declining from truth they swerved to become followers of the devil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These are they of whom the Apostle speaks: “Ye wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against principalities and powers, and the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Those invisible enemies are holpen then by unbelieving men, in whom they work in order to assail the people of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now let us see what the prophetic spirit prays may fall upon them, rather foretelling than cursing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Do thou to them,” he saith, “as unto Madian and Sisera, as unto Jabin at the brook of Kishon” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They perished at Endor, they became as the dung of the earth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All these, the history relates, were subdued and conquered by Israel, which then was the people of God: as was the case also with those whom he next mentions: “Make their princes like Oreb and Zeb, and Zebee and Salmana” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The meaning of these names is as follows: Madian is explained a perverted judgment: Sisera, shutting out of joy: Jabin, wise.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in these enemies conquered by God’s people is to be understood that wise man of whom the Apostle speaketh, “Where is the wise? where is the scribe? where is the disputer of this world?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Oreb is dryness, Zeb, wolf, Zebee, a victim, namely of the wolf; for he too has his victims; Salmana, shadow of commotion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All these agree to the evils which the people of God conquer by good.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Moreover Kishon, the torrent in which they were conquered, is explained, their hardness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Endor, where they perished, is explained, the Fountain of generation, but of the carnal generation namely, to which they were given up, and therefore perished, not heeding the regeneration which leadeth unto life, where they shall neither marry nor be given in marriage, for they shall die no more.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Rightly then it is said of these: “they became as the dung of the earth,” in that nothing was produced of them but fruitfulness of the earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As then all these were in figure conquered by the people of God, as figures, so he prays that those other enemies may be conquered in truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “All their princes, who said, Let us take to ourselves the sanctuary of God in possession” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is that vain noise, with which, as said above, Thy enemies have made a murmuring.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what must be understood by “the sanctuary of God,” except the temple of God? as saith the Apostle: “For the temple of God is holy, which temple ye are.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what else do the enemies aim at, but to take into possession, that is, to make subject to themselves the temple of God, that it may give in to their ungodly wills?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what follows?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My God, make them like unto a wheel” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is fitly taken as meaning that they should be constant in nothing that they think; but I think it may also be rightly explained, make them like unto a wheel, because a wheel is lifted up on the part of what is behind, is thrown down on the part of what is in front; and so it happens to all the enemies of the people of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this is not a wish, but a prophecy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He adds: “as the stubble in the face of the wind.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By face he means presence; for what face hath the wind, which has no bodily features, being only a motion, in that it is a kind of wave of air?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it is put for temptation, by which light and vain hearts are hurried away.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This levity, by which consent is easily given to what is evil, is followed by severe torment; therefore he proceeds:—

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Like as the fire that burneth up the wood, and as the flame that consumeth the mountains” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 14): “so shalt Thou persecute them with Thy tempest, and in Thy anger shalt disturb them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wood, he saith, for its barrenness, mountains for their loftiness; for such are the enemies of God’s people, barren of righteousness, full of pride.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When he says, “fire” and “flame,” he means to repeat under another term, the idea of God judging and punishing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in saying, “with Thy tempest,” he means, as he goes on to explain, “Thy anger:” and the former expression, “Thou shalt persecute,” answers to, “Thou shalt disturb.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We must take care, however, to understand, that the anger of God is free from any turbulent emotion; for His anger is an expression for His just method of taking vengeance: as the law might be said to be angry when its ministers are moved to punish by its sanction.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Fill their faces with shame, and they shall seek Thy name, O Lord” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Good and desirable is this which he prophesieth for them: and he would not prophesy thus, unless there were even in that company of the enemies of God’s people, some men of such kind that this would be granted to them before the last judgment: for now they are mixed together, and this is the body of the enemies, in respect of the envy whereby they rival the people of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And now, where they can, they make a noise and lift up their head: but severally, not universally as they will do at the end of the world, when the last judgment is about to fall.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it is the same body, even in those who out of this number shall believe and pass into another body (for the faces of these are filled with shame, that they may seek the name of the Lord), as well as in those others who persevere unto the end in the same wickedness, who are made as stubble before the wind, and are consumed like a wood and barren mountains.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To these he again returns, saying, “They shall blush and be vexed for ever and ever” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For those are not vexed for ever and ever who seek the name of the Lord, but having respect unto the shame of their sins, they are vexed for this purpose, that they may seek the name of the Lord, through which they may be no more vexed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Again, he returns to these last, who in the same company of enemies are to be made ashamed for this purpose, that they may not be ashamed for ever: and for this purpose to be destroyed in as far as they are wicked, that being made good they may be found alive for ever.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For having said of them, “Let them be ashamed and perish,” he instantly adds, “and let them know that Thy name is the Lord, Thou art only the Most Highest in all the earth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Coming to this knowledge, let them be so confounded as to please God: let them so perish, as that they may abide.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let them know,” he says, “that Thy name is the Lord:” as if whoever else are called lords are named so not truly but by falsehood, for they rule but as servants, and compared with the true Lord are not lords; as it is said, I Am that I Am: as if those things which are made are not, compared with Him by whom they are made.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He adds, “Thou only art the Most Highest in all the earth:” or, as other copies have it, “over all the earth;” as it might be said, in all the heaven, or over all the heaven: but he used the latter word in preference, to depress the pride of earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For earth ceaseth to be proud, that is, man ceaseth, to whom it was said, “Thou art dust;” and “why is earth and ashes proud?” when he saith that the Lord is the Most Highest above all the earth, that is, that no man’s thoughts avail against those “who are called according to His purpose,” and of whom it is said, “If God is for us, who can be against us?”
